title: Introduction to misl
text: misl implements Multiple Imputation Super Learning, flexible approach handling missing data described : Carpenito T, Manjourides J. (2022) MISL: Multiple imputation super learning. Statistical Methods Medical Research. 31(10):1904–1915. doi: 10.1177/09622802221104238 Rather relying single parametric imputation model, misl builds stacked ensemble machine learning algorithms incomplete column, producing well-calibrated imputations continuous, binary, categorical, ordered categorical variables.

title: Introduction to misl
code: # Install from GitHub remotes::install_github("JustinManjourides/misl")  # Optional backend packages for additional learners install.packages(c("ranger", "xgboost", "earth", "MASS"))

title: Introduction to misl
text: simulate small dataset four types incomplete variables demonstrate misl across supported outcome types.
code: library(misl)  set.seed(42) n <- 300  sim_data <- data.frame(   # Continuous predictors (always observed)   age    = round(rnorm(n, mean = 50, sd = 12)),   bmi    = round(rnorm(n, mean = 26, sd = 4), 1),   # Continuous outcome with missingness   sbp    = round(120 + 0.4 * rnorm(n, mean = 50, sd = 12) +                        0.3 * rnorm(n, mean = 26, sd = 4) + rnorm(n, sd = 10)),   # Binary outcome with missingness (0 = no, 1 = yes)   smoker = rbinom(n, 1, prob = 0.3),   # Unordered categorical outcome with missingness   group  = factor(sample(c("A", "B", "C"), n, replace = TRUE,                          prob = c(0.4, 0.35, 0.25))),   # Ordered categorical outcome with missingness   health = factor(sample(c("Poor", "Fair", "Good", "Excellent"), n,                          replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 0.2)),                   levels  = c("Poor", "Fair", "Good", "Excellent"),                   ordered = TRUE) )  # Introduce missing values sim_data[sample(n, 40), "sbp"]    <- NA sim_data[sample(n, 30), "smoker"] <- NA sim_data[sample(n, 30), "group"]  <- NA sim_data[sample(n, 30), "health"] <- NA  # Summarise missingness sapply(sim_data, function(x) sum(is.na(x))) #>    age    bmi    sbp smoker  group health  #>      0      0     40     30     30     30

title: Introduction to misl
text: Use list_learners() see available named learners, optionally filtered outcome type:
code: knitr::kable(list_learners()) knitr::kable(list_learners("ordinal"))

title: Introduction to misl
text: primary function misl(). Supply dataset specify: m – number multiply imputed datasets create maxit – number Gibbs sampling iterations per dataset con_method, bin_method, cat_method, ord_method – learners outcome type Running misl() single named learner per outcome type fastest option well suited exploratory work. Note ordered factors automatically detected routed ord_method: misl() returns list length m. element contains: $datasets – fully imputed tibble $trace – long-format tibble convergence statistics
code: misl_imp <- misl(   sim_data,   m          = 5,   maxit      = 5,   con_method = "glm",   bin_method = "glm",   cat_method = "multinom_reg",   ord_method = "polr",   seed       = 42,   quiet      = TRUE ) # Number of imputed datasets length(misl_imp)  # Confirm no missing values remain anyNA(misl_imp[[1]]$datasets)  # Confirm ordered factor is preserved is.ordered(misl_imp[[1]]$datasets$health) levels(misl_imp[[1]]$datasets$health)

title: Introduction to misl
text: addition built-named learners, misl v2.0 accepts parsnip-compatible model spec directly. allows use learner available tidymodels ecosystem without waiting added built-registry.

title: Introduction to misl
text: Simply pass parsnip model spec place (alongside) named string. misl automatically enforce correct mode (regression vs classification) regardless set spec:
code: library(parsnip)  # A random forest with custom hyperparameters custom_rf <- rand_forest(trees = 500, mtry = 3) |>   set_engine("ranger")  misl_custom <- misl(   sim_data,   m          = 5,   maxit      = 5,   con_method = list(custom_rf),   bin_method = list(custom_rf),   cat_method = "multinom_reg",   ord_method = "polr",   seed       = 42,   quiet      = TRUE )

what: Mixing named learners and custom specs
title: Introduction to misl
text: Named strings parsnip specs can freely mixed list. multiple learners supplied, misl uses cross-validation build stacked ensemble:
code: library(parsnip)  # Mix a named learner with a custom tuned spec custom_xgb <- boost_tree(trees = 200, learn_rate = 0.05) |>   set_engine("xgboost")  misl_mixed <- misl(   sim_data,   m          = 5,   maxit      = 5,   con_method = list("glm", custom_xgb),   bin_method = list("glm", custom_xgb),   cat_method = list("multinom_reg", "rand_forest"),   ord_method = list("polr", "rand_forest"),   cv_folds   = 3,   seed       = 42 )

what: Using a learner not in the built-in registry
title: Introduction to misl
text: parsnip-supported engine can used. example, support vector machine via kernlab package:
code: library(parsnip)  # SVM - not in the built-in registry but works via parsnip svm_spec <- svm_rbf() |>   set_engine("kernlab")  misl_svm <- misl(   sim_data,   m          = 5,   maxit      = 5,   con_method = list("glm", svm_spec),   bin_method = list("glm", svm_spec),   cat_method = "multinom_reg",   ord_method = "polr",   cv_folds   = 3,   seed       = 42 )

what: Ordinal outcomes and the polr learner
title: Introduction to misl
text: ordered categorical variables, misl automatically detects ordered factors routes ord_method. default learner "polr" (proportional odds logistic regression MASS package), respects ordering levels:
code: # Ensure your ordered variable is an ordered factor sim_data$health <- factor(sim_data$health,   levels  = c("Poor", "Fair", "Good", "Excellent"),   ordered = TRUE )  misl_ordinal <- misl(   sim_data,   m          = 5,   maxit      = 5,   con_method = "glm",   bin_method = "glm",   cat_method = "multinom_reg",   ord_method = "polr",   # proportional odds model for ordered categories   seed       = 42,   quiet      = TRUE )  # Imputed values respect the ordering is.ordered(misl_ordinal[[1]]$datasets$health) levels(misl_ordinal[[1]]$datasets$health)

title: Introduction to misl
text: multiple learners supplied (whether named strings, parsnip specs, mix), misl uses cross-validation learn optimal ensemble weights via stacks package. Use cv_folds reduce number folds speed computation:
code: misl_stack <- misl(   sim_data,   m          = 5,   maxit      = 5,   con_method = c("glm", "rand_forest"),   bin_method = c("glm", "rand_forest"),   cat_method = c("multinom_reg", "rand_forest"),   ord_method = c("polr", "rand_forest"),   cv_folds   = 3,   seed       = 42 )

title: Introduction to misl
text: imputation, fit analysis model m datasets pool results using Rubin’s rules. implement pooling manually using base R: full implementation Rubin’s rules including degrees freedom p-values, mice package provides pool() can used directly misl output:
code: # Fit a linear model to each imputed dataset models <- lapply(misl_imp, function(imp) {   lm(sbp ~ age + bmi + smoker + group + health, data = imp$datasets) })  # Pool point estimates and standard errors using Rubin's rules m       <- length(models) ests    <- sapply(models, function(fit) coef(fit)) vars    <- sapply(models, function(fit) diag(vcov(fit)))  Q_bar   <- rowMeans(ests)                          # pooled estimate U_bar   <- rowMeans(vars)                          # within-imputation variance B       <- apply(ests, 1, var)                     # between-imputation variance T_total <- U_bar + (1 + 1 / m) * B                # total variance  pooled <- data.frame(   term      = names(Q_bar),   estimate  = round(Q_bar, 4),   std.error = round(sqrt(T_total), 4),   conf.low  = round(Q_bar - 1.96 * sqrt(T_total), 4),   conf.high = round(Q_bar + 1.96 * sqrt(T_total), 4) ) print(pooled) library(mice) pooled_mice <- summary(pool(models), conf.int = TRUE)

title: Introduction to misl
text: plot_misl_trace() function plots mean standard deviation imputed values across iterations given variable, one line per imputed dataset. Stable traces mix well across datasets indicate convergence. Note trace statistics computed continuous numeric binary columns — available categorical ordinal columns. Stable traces mix well across datasets indicate algorithm converged.
code: # Plot mean of imputed sbp values across iterations for each dataset plot_misl_trace(misl_imp, variable = "sbp", ylab = "Mean imputed sbp (mm Hg)") # Plot the standard deviation instead plot_misl_trace(misl_imp, variable = "sbp", statistic = "sd")

title: Introduction to misl
text: m imputed datasets generated independently can run parallel using future framework. Set parallel plan calling misl(): limit number cores: largest speedup comes running m datasets simultaneously. Check many cores available :
code: library(future)  # Use all available cores plan(multisession)  misl_parallel <- misl(   sim_data,   m          = 10,   maxit      = 5,   con_method = c("glm", "rand_forest"),   bin_method = c("glm", "rand_forest"),   cat_method = c("multinom_reg", "rand_forest"),   ord_method = c("polr", "rand_forest"),   seed       = 42 )  # Always reset the plan when done plan(sequential) plan(multisession, workers = 4) parallel::detectCores()

what: misl: Multiple Imputation by Super Learning
title: misl: Multiple Imputation by Super Learning
text: Note: package currently experimental active development. API may change. Feedback bug reports welcome via GitHub Issues.

title: misl: Multiple Imputation by Super Learning
text: misl implements Multiple Imputation Super Learning (MISL), flexible approach handling missing data uses stacked ensemble machine learning algorithms impute missing values across continuous, binary, categorical variables. Rather relying single parametric imputation model, MISL builds super learner incomplete variable using tidymodels framework, combining learners linear/logistic regression, random forests, gradient boosted trees, MARS produce well-calibrated imputations. method described : Carpenito T, Manjourides J. (2022) MISL: Multiple imputation super learning. Statistical Methods Medical Research. 31(10):1904–1915. doi: 10.1177/09622802221104238

title: misl: Multiple Imputation by Super Learning
text: misl yet CRAN. Install development version GitHub: following backend packages optional recommended:
code: # install.packages("remotes") remotes::install_github("JustinManjourides/misl") install.packages(c("ranger", "xgboost", "earth"))

title: misl: Multiple Imputation by Super Learning
code: library(misl)  # Introduce missingness into a dataset set.seed(42) n <- 200 demo_data <- data.frame(   age    = rnorm(n, mean = 50, sd = 10),   weight = rnorm(n, mean = 70, sd = 15),   smoker = rbinom(n, 1, 0.3),   group  = factor(sample(c("A", "B", "C"), n, replace = TRUE)) ) demo_data[sample(n, 20), "age"]    <- NA demo_data[sample(n, 15), "weight"] <- NA demo_data[sample(n, 10), "smoker"] <- NA demo_data[sample(n, 10), "group"]  <- NA  # Run MISL with default settings misl_imp <- misl(   demo_data,   m      = 5,   maxit  = 5,   con_method = c("glm", "rand_forest"),   bin_method = c("glm", "rand_forest"),   cat_method = c("rand_forest", "multinom_reg") )  # Each of the m imputed datasets is accessible via: completed_data <- misl_imp[[1]]$datasets  # Trace plots can be used to inspect convergence: trace <- misl_imp[[1]]$trace

title: misl: Multiple Imputation by Super Learning
text: Imputation across m datasets parallelised via future framework. enable parallel execution, set plan calling misl():
code: library(future) plan(multisession, workers = 4)  misl_imp <- misl(demo_data, m = 5, maxit = 5)  plan(sequential)  # reset when done

title: misl: Multiple Imputation by Super Learning
code: # View all available learners list_learners()  # Filter by outcome type list_learners("continuous") list_learners("categorical")  # Show only installed learners list_learners(installed_only = TRUE)

title: List available learners for MISL imputation — list_learners
text: outcome_type One "continuous", "binomial", "categorical", "ordinal", "" (default). installed_only TRUE, learners whose backend package already installed returned. Default FALSE.

title: List available learners for MISL imputation — list_learners
text: tibble columns learner, description, package, installed, outcome-type support flags (outcome_type = "").

title: List available learners for MISL imputation — list_learners
code: list_learners() #> Note: 'polr' cannot currently be stacked with other ordinal learners. When 'polr' is supplied alongside other learners in ord_method, it will be used as the sole ordinal learner and others will be ignored. Full stacking support for ordinal outcomes is planned for a future release. #> # A tibble: 6 × 8 #>   learner  description continuous binomial categorical ordinal package installed #>   <chr>    <chr>       <lgl>      <lgl>    <lgl>       <lgl>   <chr>   <lgl>     #> 1 glm      Linear / l… TRUE       TRUE     FALSE       FALSE   stats   TRUE      #> 2 mars     Multivaria… TRUE       TRUE     FALSE       FALSE   earth   TRUE      #> 3 multino… Multinomia… FALSE      FALSE    TRUE        FALSE   nnet    TRUE      #> 4 polr     Proportion… FALSE      FALSE    FALSE       TRUE    MASS    TRUE      #> 5 rand_fo… Random for… TRUE       TRUE     TRUE        TRUE    ranger  TRUE      #> 6 boost_t… Gradient b… TRUE       TRUE     TRUE        TRUE    xgboost TRUE      list_learners("continuous") #> # A tibble: 4 × 4 #>   learner     description                              package installed #>   <chr>       <chr>                                    <chr>   <lgl>     #> 1 glm         Linear / logistic regression             stats   TRUE      #> 2 mars        Multivariate adaptive regression splines earth   TRUE      #> 3 rand_forest Random forest                            ranger  TRUE      #> 4 boost_tree  Gradient boosted trees                   xgboost TRUE      list_learners("ordinal") #> Note: 'polr' cannot currently be stacked with other ordinal learners. When 'polr' is supplied alongside other learners in ord_method, it will be used as the sole ordinal learner and others will be ignored. Full stacking support for ordinal outcomes is planned for a future release. #> # A tibble: 3 × 4 #>   learner     description                           package installed #>   <chr>       <chr>                                 <chr>   <lgl>     #> 1 polr        Proportional odds logistic regression MASS    TRUE      #> 2 rand_forest Random forest                         ranger  TRUE      #> 3 boost_tree  Gradient boosted trees                xgboost TRUE      list_learners("categorical", installed_only = TRUE) #> # A tibble: 3 × 4 #>   learner      description            package installed #>   <chr>        <chr>                  <chr>   <lgl>     #> 1 multinom_reg Multinomial regression nnet    TRUE      #> 2 rand_forest  Random forest          ranger  TRUE      #> 3 boost_tree   Gradient boosted trees xgboost TRUE

what: MISL: Multiple Imputation by Super Learning (v2.0) — misl
title: MISL: Multiple Imputation by Super Learning (v2.0) — misl
text: Imputes missing values using multiple imputation super learning.

title: MISL: Multiple Imputation by Super Learning (v2.0) — misl
code: misl(   dataset,   m = 5,   maxit = 5,   seed = NA,   con_method = c("glm", "rand_forest", "boost_tree"),   bin_method = c("glm", "rand_forest", "boost_tree"),   cat_method = c("rand_forest", "boost_tree"),   ord_method = c("polr", "rand_forest", "boost_tree"),   cv_folds = 5,   ignore_predictors = NA,   quiet = TRUE )

title: MISL: Multiple Imputation by Super Learning (v2.0) — misl
text: dataset dataframe matrix containing incomplete data. Missing values represented NA. m number multiply imputed datasets create. Default 5. maxit number iterations per imputed dataset. Default 5. seed Integer seed reproducibility, NA skip. Default NA. con_method Character vector learner IDs, list parsnip model specs, mixed list , continuous columns. Default c("glm", "rand_forest", "boost_tree"). bin_method Character vector learner IDs, list parsnip model specs, mixed list , binary columns (values must 0/1/NA two-level factor). Default c("glm", "rand_forest", "boost_tree"). cat_method Character vector learner IDs, list parsnip model specs, mixed list , unordered categorical columns. Default c("rand_forest", "boost_tree"). ord_method Character vector learner IDs, list parsnip model specs, mixed list , ordered categorical columns. Default c("polr", "rand_forest", "boost_tree"). cv_folds Integer number cross-validation folds used stacking multiple learners. Reducing (e.g. 3) speeds computation small cost ensemble accuracy. Default 5. Ignored single learner supplied. ignore_predictors Character vector column names exclude predictors. Default NA. quiet Suppress console progress messages. Default TRUE.

title: MISL: Multiple Imputation by Super Learning (v2.0) — misl
text: list m named lists, : datasets fully imputed tibble. trace long-format tibble mean/sd trace statistics per iteration, convergence inspection.

title: MISL: Multiple Imputation by Super Learning (v2.0) — misl
text: Built-named learners (see list_learners()): "glm"          - base R (logistic binary, linear continuous) "rand_forest"  - ranger "boost_tree"   - xgboost "mars"         - earth "multinom_reg" - nnet (unordered categorical ) "polr"         - MASS (ordered categorical ) parsnip-compatible model spec can also passed directly via *_method arguments. Named strings parsnip specs can mixed list: mode (regression vs classification) always enforced misl regardless set spec.
code: library(parsnip) misl(data,   con_method = list(     "glm",     rand_forest(trees = 500) |> set_engine("ranger")   ) )

title: MISL: Multiple Imputation by Super Learning (v2.0) — misl
text: Imputation across m datasets parallelised via future.apply. enable parallel execution, set future plan calling misl():
code: library(future) plan(multisession, workers = 4) result <- misl(data, m = 5) plan(sequential)

title: MISL: Multiple Imputation by Super Learning (v2.0) — misl
code: # Using named learners (same as v1.0) set.seed(1) n <- 100 demo_data <- data.frame(x1 = rnorm(n), x2 = rnorm(n), y = rnorm(n)) demo_data[sample(n, 10), "y"] <- NA misl_imp <- misl(demo_data, m = 2, maxit = 2, con_method = "glm")  # Using a custom parsnip spec if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ library(parsnip) misl_imp <- misl(   demo_data, m = 2, maxit = 2,   con_method = list(     "glm",     rand_forest(trees = 500) |> set_engine("ranger")   ) ) } # }

what: Plot trace statistics from a MISL imputation — plot_misl_trace
title: Plot trace statistics from a MISL imputation — plot_misl_trace
text: Plots mean standard deviation imputed values across iterations incomplete variables, paginated grids 3 variables per page. Stable traces mix well across datasets indicate convergence. Note trace statistics computed continuous numeric binary columns – categorical ordinal columns excluded automatically.

title: Plot trace statistics from a MISL imputation — plot_misl_trace
code: plot_misl_trace(misl_result, ncol = 2, nrow = 3)

title: Plot trace statistics from a MISL imputation — plot_misl_trace
text: misl_result list returned misl(). ncol Number columns per page. Default 2. nrow Number rows per page. Default 3.

title: Plot trace statistics from a MISL imputation — plot_misl_trace
text: Invisibly returns long-format trace data frame used plotting.

title: Plot trace statistics from a MISL imputation — plot_misl_trace
code: set.seed(1) n <- 100 demo_data <- data.frame(x1 = rnorm(n), x2 = rnorm(n), y = rnorm(n)) demo_data[sample(n, 10), "y"] <- NA misl_imp <- misl(demo_data, m = 3, maxit = 3, con_method = "glm") plot_misl_trace(misl_imp)

title: misl 2.0.0
text: CRAN release: 2026-04-08

title: misl 2.0.0
text: misl() now accepts parsnip-compatible model spec directly via *_method arguments, allowing learner tidymodels ecosystem used without requiring package update. Named strings parsnip specs can freely mixed list. New ord_method argument misl() ordered categorical (ordinal) outcomes. Ordered factors automatically detected routed ord_method. New built-learner "polr" (proportional odds logistic regression via MASS::polr()) ordinal outcomes. Note "polr" currently stacked ordinal learners — supplied alongside learners ord_method, "polr" used sole ordinal learner others ignored warning. Full stacking support ordinal outcomes planned future release. New plot_misl_trace() function visualising convergence imputed values across iterations, one line per imputed dataset. list_learners() now accepts "ordinal" valid outcome_type filter includes new ordinal support column.

title: misl 2.0.0
text: cv_folds argument now correctly passed internal .fit_super_learner() function. Previously value accepted misl() ignored, 5 folds always used regardless user’s setting. preprocessing recipe now built full observed data shared across bootstrap full fits continuous outcomes. Previously separate recipes built fit, meaning step_zv() step_nzv() drop different predictors two fits produce inconsistent PMM donor selection. Two-level factors (e.g. "Yes"/"") now correctly identified binomial outcomes rather categorical. Previously routed cat_method, caused incorrect imputation errors learners require numeric binary outcome. Trace statistics (mean, sd) longer computed factor-coded binary columns, preventing errors calling mean() var() factor vectors. Stacking now gracefully recovers individual learners fail cross-validation resampling. Previously single learner failure crash entire imputation. Failed learners now skipped warning, learners fail first learner used fallback.

title: misl 1.0.0
text: CRAN release: 2026-03-30 Initial CRAN submission.
